ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI for sweet potato dessert soup; closest match is the sweet potato food product class. The prepared soup form with rock sugar is not individually represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers sweet potato as a raw commodity. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for the prepared sweet potato soup dessert form; this is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for sweet potato soup dessert preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of sweet potato soup is one bowl of approximately 250 g, as commonly served at Singaporean dessert stalls and hawker centres.

nutrient_source.title: Sweet Potato Soup — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sweet Potato Soup (F02050020). All per-100g values are taken from the canonical HPB record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 61 (medium) is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled sweet potato. The soup preparation with rock sugar is analogous to boiled sweet potato in a liquid medium; the added rock sugar marginally elevates the effective GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (61 × 14.8 × 2.5) / 100 = 22.5, rounded to 22.2 using 250 g serving and 14.8 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Added rock sugar with dietary fibre below 2 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium is low at 9 mg/100 g and is not a concern; however, the added rock sugar (contributing 6.6 g sugar/100 g) combined with dietary fibre below the 2 g/100 g threshold makes this dessert preparation moderately unsuitable for regular hypertension management — prepare without added sugar to improve suitability.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added rock sugar elevating free sugar to 6.6 g/100 g with medium GI of 61 and moderate glycemic load
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combination of 6.6 g free sugar per 100 g from added rock sugar, a medium GI of 61, and a glycemic load of approximately 22 per 250 g serving makes this dessert unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; an unsweetened version using only the natural sweetness of sweet potato would substantially reduce glycemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low fat (0.1 g/100 g), low sodium (9 mg/100 g), and negligible saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The fat and sodium profiles are highly favourable for cardiovascular health; the primary concern is the added rock sugar contributing 6.6 g free sugar per 100 g, which should be minimised or eliminated in a heart-healthy preparation, but the overall lipid and sodium profile does not pose a cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sweet potato beta-carotene and optional ginger gingerols provide anti-inflammatory benefit
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sweet potato contributes beta-carotene and vitamin C with anti-inflammatory properties; if ginger is included, gingerols provide evidence-based anti-inflammatory benefit relevant to RA, though added sugar should be reduced to maximise this effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sweet Potato Soup (番薯糖水, Fan Shu Tang Shui) is a traditional Singaporean and Cantonese-Chinese dessert soup made by simmering chunks of sweet potato in water with rock sugar until tender and lightly sweetened. The dish is commonly served warm or at room temperature as a dessert or snack at hawker centres and dessert stalls across Singapore. The texture is soft and yielding, with a naturally starchy, mildly sweet flavour enhanced by the dissolved rock sugar. Ginger slices are frequently added to provide a gentle warming note and modest anti-inflammatory benefit. Per 100 g, the soup provides 62 kcal, 14.8 g carbohydrate, 6.6 g sugar (largely from added rock sugar), 1.9 g dietary fibre, 0.6 g protein, and only 0.1 g fat, with a low sodium content of 9 mg. The glycemic index is approximately 61 (medium), yielding a glycemic load of around 22 per 250 g bowl. Clinically, the added rock sugar makes this dessert unsuitable for regular consumption by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or those managing hypertension through sugar restriction; an unsweetened preparation using only the natural sweetness of sweet potato would be substantially more suitable. The very low fat and sodium profile is favourable for cardiovascular health. Sweet potato contributes beta-carotene, vitamin C, calcium (8 mg/100 g), and iron (0.5 mg/100 g).
common_user_queries: Can diabetics eat sweet potato soup?
How many calories in sweet potato soup?
What is fan shu tang shui?
Is sweet potato soup good for high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo canonical database entry F02050020 with confidence 0.95. GI value derived from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled sweet potato as an analogous estimate. All mandatory multilingual names are present and verified.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this sweet potato soup preparation is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F02050020. potassium_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB source for this specific preparation. saturated_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible for this very low-fat preparation per HPB source). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — no added fats in this preparation). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero — plant-based preparation with no animal-derived ingredients). GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled sweet potato; the soup form with rock sugar is treated as equivalent to boiled sweet potato in a liquid medium. The rheumatoid arthritis caution referencing nightshades in the original input has been removed as sweet potato (Ipomoea batatas) is not a nightshade (Solanaceae) — it belongs to Convolvulaceae and does not contain solanine.
